questions.question: Sound differs from X-radiation in one fundamental way that shapes all of UT practice. Sound:
options: travels faster in a vacuum than in steel
is electromagnetic radiation of very low energy
requires a material medium and cannot cross a vacuum
cannot be reflected at material boundaries
questions.explanation: Sound is mechanical vibration carried particle-to-particle; it needs a solid, liquid, or gas. This is why couplant is required and why internal air gaps reflect nearly all the energy.

questions.question: Paul Langevin's World War I echo-ranging work mattered to later flaw detection because it:
options: proved that sound cannot travel in water
showed piezoelectric transducers could transmit ultrasonic pulses and receive their echoes
produced the first commercial weld-inspection flaw detector
eliminated the need for a couplant between probe and part
questions.explanation: Langevin's quartz transducers for submarine detection were the first serious engineering use of ultrasonic pulses and echoes — the direct ancestor of pulse-echo flaw detection and sonar.

questions.question: Sokolov's 1929 through-transmission technique was limited compared with pulse-echo because it:
options: required access to both surfaces and gave no flaw-depth information
used ionizing radiation requiring exclusion zones
could not detect any internal defects at all
questions.explanation: Through-transmission watches for a defect's 'shadow' at a receiver on the far face: it needs two-side access and alignment, and a shadow says nothing about reflector depth.

questions.question: Which group lists only HIGH-power applications of ultrasonic energy?
options: Weld inspection, corrosion surveys, bond testing
Ultrasonic cleaning, plastic welding, machining of brittle materials
Thickness gauging, lamination scanning, rail inspection
questions.explanation: Cleaning, welding, and machining deliberately change the material with high-power ultrasound; all the other lists are low-power applications that only gather information.

questions.question: A 10 MHz longitudinal wave in aluminum (6,320 m/s) has a wavelength of about:
questions.explanation: λ = v ÷ f = 6.32 mm/µs ÷ 10 MHz = 0.632 mm. Velocity in mm/µs divided by frequency in MHz gives wavelength in millimeters directly.

questions.question: An echo drops from 80% FSH to 10% FSH. The change in decibels is closest to:
questions.explanation: The ratio is 10/80 = 1/8, which is three halvings: −6 −6 −6 = −18 dB (20 × log₁₀(0.125) ≈ −18.1 dB).

questions.question: A 70° shear-wave probe is used on 12 mm plate (tan 70° = 2.747). The full skip distance is about:
questions.explanation: Skip = 2 × T × tan θ = 2 × 12 × 2.747 ≈ 65.9 mm. Forgetting the factor of two gives the 33 mm distractor.

questions.question: Steel's longitudinal velocity of 5,920 m/s expressed for a microsecond time base is:
questions.explanation: 5,920 m/s = 5,920,000 mm per 1,000,000 µs = 5.92 mm/µs — the form used for quick range and wavelength arithmetic on the instrument.

questions.question: In an SNT-TC-1A-based program, the document that defines required training hours, exam grades, vision tests, and recertification intervals is:
options: the construction code for the part being tested
questions.explanation: SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice; each employer converts its guidelines into a binding written practice that governs qualification and certification details.

questions.question: A new contract requires UT of a component type the shop has never examined. Designating the technique and approving the procedure falls to:
questions.explanation: Establishing and approving procedures and designating the method and technique for a job are Level III responsibilities; Level II then runs examinations under that approved procedure.
